questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a chemical change?
questions.hint: Chemical changes produce new substances with different properties.
questions.explanation: Rusting of iron forms iron oxide, a new substance with different properties, making it a chemical change. Melting ice, dissolving sugar, and evaporating alcohol are physical changes where no new substance is formed.

source_trace.content_anchor: rusting of iron articles in moist air is a chemical change

explanations_tiered.definition: A chemical change results in the formation of one or more new substances with different chemical compositions.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Chemical changes are irreversible and produce new substances. Physical changes only alter physical state or appearance without changing chemical identity.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Rusting changes iron into a reddish-brown crumbly substance (rust). You can't turn rust back into shiny iron easily \u2014 that's a chemical change.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Rusting of iron: \(4\mathrm{Fe} + 3\mathrm{O_2} \rightarrow 2\mathrm{Fe_2O_3}\). Iron oxide (rust) has completely different properties from iron metal, so this is a chemical change.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Chemical changes involve bond breaking and formation. In rusting, Fe atoms lose electrons to O\u2082, forming ionic Fe\u00b3\u207a and O\u00b2\u207b ions in a lattice. This is irreversible under ambient conditions.
reasoning_steps: Determine if a new substance is formed
Check if the change is reversible
Rusting produces iron oxide (new substance) and is irreversible
misconceptions: Dissolving is a chemical change (incorrect; it's usually physical, though some dissolution involves reaction).
All changes involving heat are chemical (incorrect; melting is physical).
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Chemical change: new substance formed. Rusting: Fe \u2192 Fe\u2082O\u2083.

glossary_entry.definition: A change that produces one or more new substances with different chemical properties.

flashcard.front: Is rusting of iron a chemical or physical change? Why?
flashcard.back: Chemical change because iron reacts with oxygen to form a new substance (iron oxide) with different properties.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Chemistry Unit 1 — Chemical vs Physical Changes
publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Which is a chemical change: rusting, melting ice, or dissolving sugar?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Rusting is a chemical change; melting ice and dissolving sugar are physical changes.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: A change that produces new substances with different chemical properties.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Chemical change: new substance formed. Rusting = chemical.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Have students make a T-chart of physical vs chemical changes with examples from daily life.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Distinguish between physical and chemical changes using examples
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Rusting produces iron oxide, a new substance, so it is a chemical change.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: What is the correct unbalanced chemical equation for the reaction where sodium reacts with chlorine to produce sodium chloride?
questions.hint: Chlorine exists as a diatomic molecule (Cl\u2082).
questions.explanation: Correct unbalanced equation: \(\mathrm{Na + Cl_2 \rightarrow NaCl}\). Option A is wrong because chlorine is diatomic (Cl\u2082, not Cl). Option C has Na\u2082 (sodium metal is monatomic), D has incorrect product formula.

source_trace.content_anchor: Hydrogen, nitrogen, oxygen, fluorine, chlorine, bromine and iodine exist as diatomic molecules

explanations_tiered.definition: A chemical equation is a shorthand representation of a chemical reaction using chemical symbols and formulas.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: When writing equations, remember that seven elements (H\u2082, N\u2082, O\u2082, F\u2082, Cl\u2082, Br\u2082, I\u2082) are diatomic. Sodium is monatomic as a metal.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Chlorine gas comes in pairs (Cl\u2082), like twins that always stick together. So in the equation, you write Cl\u2082, not Cl.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The word equation is: Sodium + Chlorine \u2192 Sodium chloride. Sodium is Na, chlorine is Cl\u2082 (diatomic), sodium chloride is NaCl. So the unbalanced equation is \(\mathrm{Na + Cl_2 \rightarrow NaCl}\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Diatomic elements (HOFBrINCl) exist as stable molecules with covalent bonds. Chlorine's ground state is Cl\u2082, not Cl atoms, because the Cl-Cl bond provides stability.
reasoning_steps: Write reactants on left, product on right with arrow
misconceptions: Chlorine is written as Cl (incorrect; it's Cl\u2082 as an element).
Sodium is written as Na\u2082 (incorrect; sodium metal is monatomic).
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Diatomic elements: H\u2082, N\u2082, O\u2082, F\u2082, Cl\u2082, Br\u2082, I\u2082. Write Na + Cl\u2082 \u2192 NaCl.

glossary_entry.definition: Using correct symbols and formulas, including diatomic molecules for seven elements.

flashcard.front: What is the correct unbalanced equation for sodium + chlorine \u2192 sodium chloride?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Write the unbalanced equation for sodium + chlorine \u2192 sodium chloride.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: An element that exists as a molecule of two atoms (H\u2082, N\u2082, O\u2082, F\u2082, Cl\u2082, Br\u2082, I\u2082).
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Memorize the seven diatomic elements: 'Have No Fear Of Ice Cold Beer' (H\u2082, N\u2082, F\u2082, O\u2082, I\u2082, Cl\u2082, Br\u2082).
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Write chemical equations using correct symbols and formulas
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Sodium is Na, chlorine is Cl\u2082 (diatomic), product is NaCl.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: Why must oxidation and reduction always occur simultaneously in a redox reaction?

options.A: Because electrons cannot be created or destroyed, only transferred
options.B: Because oxygen must always be present for oxidation to occur
options.C: Because reduction can occur without oxidation in some reactions

questions.hint: Electrons lost by one species must be gained by another.
questions.explanation: Oxidation is the loss of electrons, reduction is the gain of electrons. Electrons cannot exist freely, so electrons lost in oxidation must be gained in reduction. Option B is false (oxidation can occur without oxygen), C is false (they are paired), D is not the fundamental reason.

source_trace.content_anchor: Oxidation and reduction or redox reactions occur simultaneously in a given reaction

explanations_tiered.definition: Oxidation and reduction are complementary processes that occur together because electrons are conserved.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Electrons cannot exist independently in solution. When one species loses electrons (oxidation), another species must gain those exact electrons (reduction).
explanations_tiered.beginner: Think of giving a gift: if someone gives away a gift (loses electrons), someone else must receive it (gains electrons). You can't have a giver without a receiver.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: In \(\mathrm{Zn + Cu^{2+} \rightarrow Zn^{2+} + Cu}\), Zn loses 2 electrons (oxidation) and Cu\u00b2\u207a gains those 2 electrons (reduction). The processes are simultaneous and inseparable.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Electron conservation is a fundamental principle. The half-reactions show that electrons lost in oxidation equal electrons gained in reduction: \(\mathrm{Zn \rightarrow Zn^{2+} + 2e^-}\) and \(\mathrm{Cu^{2+} + 2e^- \rightarrow Cu}\).
reasoning_steps: Therefore, oxidation and reduction occur together
misconceptions: Oxidation always involves oxygen (incorrect; it's defined by electron loss).
Reduction can happen alone (incorrect; electron gain requires an electron source).
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Oxidation (loss) and reduction (gain) occur together. Electrons transferred, not created.

glossary_entry.definition: Oxidation and reduction occur together because electrons lost in oxidation are gained in reduction.

flashcard.front: Why must oxidation and reduction happen together?
flashcard.back: Electrons lost in oxidation must be gained in reduction; electrons cannot exist freely.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Can oxidation occur without reduction? Explain.
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: No; electrons lost in oxidation must be gained by another species in reduction.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Oxidation and reduction always occur together because electrons are transferred, not created or destroyed.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Oxidation and reduction: always together. Electrons lost = electrons gained.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use the analogy of a bank transaction: withdrawal (oxidation) requires a deposit (reduction) elsewhere.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Explain why oxidation and reduction occur together in redox reactions
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Electrons are conserved; loss of electrons (oxidation) requires gain (reduction).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.section: 1.5 Molecular and Formula Masses, the Mole Concept and Chemical Formulas
questions.question: What is the formula mass of \(\mathrm{Ca(OH)_2}\) given that Ca = 40 amu, O = 16 amu, H = 1 amu?
questions.hint: Formula mass = sum of atomic masses of all atoms in the formula unit.
questions.explanation: \(\mathrm{Ca(OH)_2}\) contains 1 Ca, 2 O, and 2 H. Calculation: \(40 + (2 \times 16) + (2 \times 1) = 40 + 32 + 2 = 74\) amu. Option A is wrong, B is close but missing one H, D would be \(\mathrm{Ca(OH)_2 \cdot H_2O}\).

calculation_steps: Formula: \(\mathrm{Ca(OH)_2}\) contains 1 Ca, 2 O, and 2 H

source_trace.section_anchor: 1.5 Molecular and Formula Masses, the Mole Concept and Chemical Formulas

explanations_tiered.definition: Formula mass is the sum of atomic masses of all atoms in a formula unit, used primarily for ionic compounds.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: For \(\mathrm{Ca(OH)_2}\), multiply each atomic mass by the subscript and sum. The subscript applies to everything inside parentheses.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Calcium hydroxide has 1 calcium, 2 oxygens, and 2 hydrogens. Add them up: 40 + 32 + 2 = 74.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Formula mass is numerically equal to molar mass in g/mol. For \(\mathrm{Ca(OH)_2}\), molar mass = 74 g/mol, used in stoichiometric calculations for reactions like \(\mathrm{Ca(OH)_2 + CO_2 \rightarrow CaCO_3 + H_2O}\).
worked_solution: Step 1: Write the formula: \(\mathrm{Ca(OH)_2}\)
Step 2: Identify number of atoms: Ca = 1, O = 2, H = 2
Step 3: Multiply atomic masses: Ca = 1 \u00d7 40 = 40, O = 2 \u00d7 16 = 32, H = 2 \u00d7 1 = 2
Step 4: Sum: 40 + 32 + 2 = 74 amu
misconceptions: Forgetting to multiply OH by 2 (giving 40 + 16 + 1 = 57 amu, a common distractor).
Using molecular mass instead of formula mass (for ionic compounds, formula mass is correct term).

glossary_entry.definition: The sum of atomic masses in one formula unit of Ca(OH)\u2082: 74 amu.

flashcard.front: Calculate the formula mass of \(\mathrm{Ca(OH)_2}\) (Ca=40, O=16, H=1).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is the formula mass of \(\mathrm{Ca(OH)_2}\)? (Ca=40, O=16, H=1)
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The sum of atomic masses of all atoms in a formula unit of an ionic compound.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize that parentheses indicate the subscript multiplies everything inside.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Calculate formula mass of ionic compounds

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.section: 1.5 Molecular and Formula Masses, the Mole Concept and Chemical Formulas
questions.question: How many atoms are present in 1 mole of carbon-12?
questions.hint: One mole contains Avogadro's number of particles.
questions.explanation: One mole of any substance contains exactly \(6.022 \times 10^{23}\) entities (Avogadro's number). For carbon-12, 1 mole = \(6.022 \times 10^{23}\) atoms. Option B is for 2 moles, C is half a mole, D is the mass of one amu in grams.

calculation_steps: 1 mole of any substance contains Avogadro's number of particles
Therefore, 1 mole of C-12 contains \(6.022 \times 10^{23}\) atoms

source_trace.section_anchor: 1.5 Molecular and Formula Masses, the Mole Concept and Chemical Formulas
source_trace.content_anchor: 12 g of carbon-12 contains 6.022 \u00d7 10^{23} atoms. This number is known as Avogadro's number

explanations_tiered.definition: One mole contains Avogadro's number (\(6.022 \times 10^{23}\)) of particles, whether atoms, molecules, or ions.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The mole is a counting unit that connects the microscopic world (atoms) to the macroscopic world (grams). 1 mole of any element contains \(6.022 \times 10^{23}\) atoms.
explanations_tiered.beginner: A mole is like a 'chemist's dozen' but much bigger. One dozen = 12, one mole = \(6.022 \times 10^{23}\) (602 sextillion)!
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Avogadro's number \((6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ mol}^{-1})\) is defined as the number of carbon-12 atoms in exactly 12 grams of pure carbon-12. Therefore, 1 mole of carbon-12 contains exactly that many atoms.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Since the 2019 redefinition of SI base units, Avogadro's constant is exactly \(6.02214076 \times 10^{23} \text{ mol}^{-1}\). For most calculations, \(6.022 \times 10^{23}\) is sufficiently accurate.
reasoning_steps: Recall definition of mole: amount containing Avogadro's number of particles
misconceptions: Avogadro's number changes with the substance (incorrect; it's a constant).
One mole of carbon-12 has a different number of atoms than one mole of any other element (incorrect; it's the same number of atoms but different mass).
explanations_tiered.revision_note: 1 mole = \(6.022 \times 10^{23}\) particles. 1 mol C-12 atoms = \(6.022 \times 10^{23}\) atoms.

glossary_entry.definition: The amount of carbon-12 containing Avogadro's number (\(6.022 \times 10^{23}\)) of atoms.

flashcard.front: How many atoms are in 1 mole of carbon-12?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: How many atoms are in 1 mole of carbon-12?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The number of particles in one mole: \(6.022 \times 10^{23}\).
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Compare to a ream of paper (500 sheets) or a dozen (12) to explain the concept of a counting unit.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Define the mole and Avogadro's number
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. One mole always contains \(6.022 \times 10^{23}\) particles.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.section: 1.5 Molecular and Formula Masses, the Mole Concept and Chemical Formulas
questions.subsection: 1.5.3 Chemical Formulas - Empirical and Molecular Formulas
questions.question: What is the difference between an empirical formula and a molecular formula?

options.A: Empirical formula shows simplest whole-number ratio; molecular formula shows actual number of atoms
options.B: Empirical formula shows actual number of atoms; molecular formula shows simplest ratio
options.C: Empirical formula is used only for ionic compounds; molecular formula is used only for covalent compounds
options.D: There is no difference; they are the same

questions.hint: Empirical = simplest ratio, molecular = actual numbers.
questions.explanation: Empirical formula gives the simplest whole-number ratio of atoms (e.g., CH\u2082O for glucose). Molecular formula gives the actual number of atoms in a molecule (e.g., C\u2086H\u2081\u2082O\u2086 for glucose). Option B reverses them, C is false (both can be used for covalent compounds), D is false.

source_trace.section_anchor: 1.5 Molecular and Formula Masses, the Mole Concept and Chemical Formulas
source_trace.subsection_anchor: 1.5.3 Chemical Formulas - Empirical and Molecular Formulas
source_trace.content_anchor: Empirical formula is written with the smallest ratio subscripts. Molecular formula tells the exact number of atoms

explanations_tiered.definition: The empirical formula is the simplest whole-number ratio of atoms; the molecular formula is the actual number of atoms in a molecule.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Many compounds share the same empirical formula but different molecular formulas. The molecular formula is always a whole-number multiple of the empirical formula.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Think of a recipe: empirical formula is like saying 'for every 1 apple, you need 2 slices of bread' (simplest ratio). Molecular formula is like saying 'you need 6 apples and 12 slices' (actual amounts).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: For glucose: empirical formula CH\u2082O (C:H:O = 1:2:1), molecular formula C\u2086H\u2081\u2082O\u2086 (6 times the empirical formula). For methane CH\u2084, empirical and molecular formulas are the same.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The relationship is molecular formula = (empirical formula) \u00d7 n, where n = (molecular mass) / (empirical formula mass). n is always a positive integer (1, 2, 3, \u2026).
reasoning_steps: Recall definition of empirical formula (simplest ratio)
Recall definition of molecular formula (actual atoms)
Recognize that molecular formula = n \u00d7 empirical formula
misconceptions: Empirical and molecular formulas are always different (incorrect; e.g., H\u2082O, CH\u2084).
Ionic compounds have molecular formulas (incorrect; they have formula units, not molecules).
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Empirical = simplest ratio. Molecular = actual atoms. Glucose: CH\u2082O (empirical), C\u2086H\u2081\u2082O\u2086 (molecular).

glossary_entry.definition: Empirical formula is simplest whole-number ratio; molecular formula gives actual atom counts.

flashcard.front: What is the difference between empirical and molecular formulas?
flashcard.back: Empirical shows simplest ratio; molecular shows actual number of atoms in a molecule.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is the difference between empirical and molecular formulas?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Empirical = simplest ratio; molecular = actual number of atoms in a molecule.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The simplest whole-number ratio of atoms in a compound.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Empirical = simplest ratio. Molecular = actual atoms.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use examples: H\u2082O (same), H\u2082O\u2082 (empirical HO), C\u2086H\u2081\u2082O\u2086 (empirical CH\u2082O).
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Distinguish between empirical and molecular formulas
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Empirical formula = simplest ratio; molecular formula = actual number of atoms.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.subsection: 1.6.1 Molar Ratios in Balanced Chemical Equation
questions.question: In the balanced equation \(2\mathrm{H_2} + \mathrm{O_2} \rightarrow 2\mathrm{H_2O}\), how many moles of \(\mathrm{H_2O}\) are produced from 4 moles of \(\mathrm{H_2}\)?
questions.explanation: From the balanced equation: 2 mol H\u2082 produce 2 mol H\u2082O, so the mole ratio is 1:1. Therefore, 4 mol H\u2082 produce 4 mol H\u2082O. Option A would be for 2 mol H\u2082, C and D are incorrect based on the stoichiometry.

calculation_steps: If 4 mol H\u2082 react, produce 4 mol H\u2082O

source_trace.subsection_anchor: 1.6.1 Molar Ratios in Balanced Chemical Equation
source_trace.content_anchor: 2H_2 + O_2 \u2192 2H_2O: 2 mol H_2 produce 2 mol H_2O

explanations_tiered.definition: Mole ratios from a balanced equation allow conversion between amounts of reactants and products.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The coefficients in a balanced equation give the relative number of moles. For \(2\mathrm{H_2} + \mathrm{O_2} \rightarrow 2\mathrm{H_2O}\), the ratio H\u2082:H\u2082O is 2:2, or 1:1.
explanations_tiered.beginner: The big numbers (coefficients) tell you how many molecules react. Here, 2 hydrogen molecules make 2 water molecules. So the number of hydrogen and water molecules is the same.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: From \(2\mathrm{H_2} + \mathrm{O_2} \rightarrow 2\mathrm{H_2O}\), the mole ratio \(\frac{\mathrm{H_2}}{\mathrm{H_2O}} = \frac{2}{2} = \frac{1}{1}\). Therefore, 4 mol H\u2082 produces 4 mol H\u2082O.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Using dimensional analysis: \(4\text{ mol H}_2 \times \frac{2\text{ mol H}_2O}{2\text{ mol H}_2} = 4\text{ mol H}_2O\). The mole ratio is derived directly from the stoichiometric coefficients.
worked_solution: Step 1: Write balanced equation: \(2\mathrm{H_2} + \mathrm{O_2} \rightarrow 2\mathrm{H_2O}\)
Step 2: Identify mole ratio: 2 mol H\u2082 : 2 mol H\u2082O = 1:1
Step 3: Apply to given amount: 4 mol H\u2082 \u00d7 (1 mol H\u2082O / 1 mol H\u2082) = 4 mol H\u2082O
reasoning_steps: Identify coefficients of H\u2082 (2) and H\u2082O (2)
Write ratio: 2 mol H\u2082 produces 2 mol H\u2082O
misconceptions: Using the coefficient of O\u2082 instead of H\u2082 (incorrect; the question asks about H\u2082, not O\u2082).
Assuming a 2:1 ratio between H\u2082 and H\u2082O (incorrect; both have coefficient 2).

glossary_entry.definition: In the reaction 2H\u2082 + O\u2082 \u2192 2H\u2082O, the mole ratio of H\u2082 to H\u2082O is 1:1.

flashcard.front: How many moles of H\u2082O are produced from 4 moles of H\u2082 in \(2\mathrm{H_2} + \mathrm{O_2} \rightarrow 2\mathrm{H_2O}\)?
flashcard.back: 4 moles of H\u2082O (1:1 mole ratio from coefficients).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: 4 mol H\u2082 in \(2\mathrm{H_2} + \mathrm{O_2} \rightarrow 2\mathrm{H_2O}\) produces how many mol H\u2082O?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Using coefficients from a balanced equation to convert between moles of substances.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Practice multiple problems with different mole ratios (e.g., N\u2082 + 3H\u2082 \u2192 2NH\u2083).
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Use mole ratios from balanced equations to solve stoichiometric problems
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. 2H\u2082 produces 2H\u2082O (1:1 ratio), so 4 mol H\u2082 produces 4 mol H\u2082O.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: How many grams of magnesium oxide (MgO) are produced when 24 g of magnesium reacts completely with excess oxygen? (Mg = 24 g/mol, O = 16 g/mol, MgO = 40 g/mol)
questions.hint: Write balanced equation: \(2\mathrm{Mg} + \mathrm{O_2} \rightarrow 2\mathrm{MgO}\). Then use mass-mass stoichiometry.
questions.explanation: Balanced: \(2\mathrm{Mg} + \mathrm{O_2} \rightarrow 2\mathrm{MgO}\). 2 mol Mg (48 g) produces 2 mol MgO (80 g). So 24 g Mg (1 mol) produces 40 g MgO (1 mol). Option A is half, C and D are incorrect multiples.

calculation_steps: Molar masses: Mg = 24 g/mol, MgO = 40 g/mol
From equation: 2 mol Mg (48 g) produces 2 mol MgO (80 g)
Thus 24 g Mg (1 mol) produces 40 g MgO (1 mol)

source_trace.content_anchor: Mass-mass stoichiometric calculations using balanced equations

explanations_tiered.definition: Mass-mass stoichiometry uses the balanced equation to convert mass of one substance to mass of another.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Convert given mass to moles using molar mass, use mole ratio from balanced equation, then convert moles of desired substance to mass.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Magnesium burns in oxygen to make magnesium oxide. 24 grams of magnesium (1 mole) makes 40 grams of magnesium oxide (1 mole).
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Balanced: \(2\mathrm{Mg} + \mathrm{O_2} \rightarrow 2\mathrm{MgO}\). 1 mol Mg (24 g) produces 1 mol MgO (40 g) because the mole ratio Mg:MgO = 2:2 = 1:1.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Dimensional analysis: \(24\text{ g Mg} \times \frac{1\text{ mol Mg}}{24\text{ g Mg}} \times \frac{2\text{ mol MgO}}{2\text{ mol Mg}} \times \frac{40\text{ g MgO}}{1\text{ mol MgO}} = 40\text{ g MgO}\).
worked_solution: Step 1: Balanced equation: \(2\mathrm{Mg} + \mathrm{O_2} \rightarrow 2\mathrm{MgO}\)
Step 2: Moles of Mg = 24 g / 24 g/mol = 1 mol
Step 3: Mole ratio Mg:MgO = 2:2 = 1:1, so moles MgO = 1 mol
Step 4: Mass MgO = 1 mol \u00d7 40 g/mol = 40 g
reasoning_steps: Convert moles of desired substance to mass
misconceptions: Using 1:2 ratio between Mg and MgO (incorrect; both have coefficient 2, so 1:1).
Forgetting to convert moles to mass (stopping at 1 mol MgO).
explanations_tiered.revision_note: 2Mg + O\u2082 \u2192 2MgO. 24 g Mg (1 mol) \u2192 40 g MgO (1 mol).

glossary_entry.definition: 24 g Mg produces 40 g MgO based on the 1:1 mole ratio in the balanced equation.

flashcard.front: What mass of MgO is produced from 24 g Mg (Mg=24, O=16, MgO=40)?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What mass of MgO from 24 g Mg in \(2\mathrm{Mg} + \mathrm{O_2} \rightarrow 2\mathrm{MgO}\)?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: 40 g MgO (1:1 mole ratio, 1 mol MgO = 40 g).
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Calculating mass of product from mass of reactant using balanced equation and molar masses.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: 24 g Mg \u2192 40 g MgO (1:1 mole ratio).
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Demonstrate the reaction of magnesium ribbon in air to produce bright white light and white MgO powder.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. 1 mol Mg (24 g) produces 1 mol MgO (40 g) based on 1:1 mole ratio.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: What volume of oxygen gas is needed to completely react with 44.8 L of hydrogen gas to produce water vapor, assuming all gases are at STP? (Reaction: \(2\mathrm{H_2} + \mathrm{O_2} \rightarrow 2\mathrm{H_2O}\))
questions.hint: At STP, volume ratios are the same as mole ratios (Avogadro's law).
questions.explanation: From \(2\mathrm{H_2} + \mathrm{O_2} \rightarrow 2\mathrm{H_2O}\), the volume ratio H\u2082:O\u2082 is 2:1. For 44.8 L H\u2082, O\u2082 needed = \(44.8 \times (1/2) = 22.4\) L. Option A is too small, C would be for 1:1 ratio, D would be for 2:1 reversed.

calculation_steps: Volume ratio H\u2082:O\u2082 = 2:1 (from coefficients, Avogadro's law)

source_trace.content_anchor: At STP, volume ratios equal mole ratios (Avogadro's law)

explanations_tiered.definition: Avogadro's law states that equal volumes of gases at the same T and P contain equal numbers of molecules, so volume ratios equal mole ratios.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: For gas reactions at constant temperature and pressure, the coefficients in the balanced equation give the volume ratios directly.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Gases at STP behave the same: 2 volumes of hydrogen need 1 volume of oxygen. So for 44.8 L of hydrogen, you need half that volume (22.4 L) of oxygen.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Avogadro's law: V \u221d n. Therefore, \(\frac{V_{\mathrm{O_2}}}{V_{\mathrm{H_2}}} = \frac{n_{\mathrm{O_2}}}{n_{\mathrm{H_2}}} = \frac{1}{2}\). So \(V_{\mathrm{O_2}} = 44.8 \times \frac{1}{2} = 22.4\) L.
explanations_tiered.advanced: At STP (0\u00b0C, 1 atm), 1 mole of any ideal gas occupies 22.4 L. The balanced equation shows 2 mol H\u2082 (44.8 L) reacts with 1 mol O\u2082 (22.4 L). Volume ratios are directly proportional to mole ratios.
worked_solution: Step 1: Balanced equation: \(2\mathrm{H_2} + \mathrm{O_2} \rightarrow 2\mathrm{H_2O}\)
Step 2: Mole ratio H\u2082:O\u2082 = 2:1
Step 3: At STP, volume ratio = mole ratio = 2:1
reasoning_steps: Note that Avogadro's law applies (gases at STP)
Use coefficients to get volume ratio
Multiply given volume by the ratio (desired/known)
misconceptions: Assuming 1:1 volume ratio between H\u2082 and O\u2082 (incorrect; balanced equation shows 2:1).
Using 22.4 L/mol incorrectly (not needed when given volumes, just use ratio directly).

glossary_entry.definition: Using volume ratios from balanced equations for gas reactions at constant T and P (Avogadro's law).
glossary_entry.example_equation: 44.8 L H\u2082 reacts with 22.4 L O\u2082

flashcard.front: What volume of O\u2082 reacts with 44.8 L H\u2082 in \(2\mathrm{H_2} + \mathrm{O_2} \rightarrow 2\mathrm{H_2O}\) at STP?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: 44.8 L H\u2082 at STP reacts with how many L O\u2082 in \(2\mathrm{H_2} + \mathrm{O_2} \rightarrow 2\mathrm{H_2O}\)?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Gas volume ratios equal mole ratios at constant T and P (Avogadro's law).
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Demonstrate with balloon stoichiometry: 2 H\u2082 + O\u2082 \u2192 2 H\u2082O, showing volume contraction.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Solve volume-volume stoichiometric problems using Avogadro's law
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Volume ratio H\u2082:O\u2082 = 2:1, so 44.8 L H\u2082 requires 22.4 L O\u2082.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.question: What volume of hydrogen gas at STP is produced when 6.5 g of zinc reacts completely with excess hydrochloric acid? (Zn = 65 g/mol, reaction: \(\mathrm{Zn + 2HCl \rightarrow ZnCl_2 + H_2}\))
questions.hint: First find moles of Zn, then use mole ratio Zn:H\u2082 = 1:1, then convert to volume at STP (22.4 L/mol).
questions.explanation: Moles Zn = 6.5 g / 65 g/mol = 0.1 mol. From balanced equation, 1 mol Zn produces 1 mol H\u2082, so moles H\u2082 = 0.1 mol. Volume at STP = 0.1 mol \u00d7 22.4 L/mol = 2.24 L. Option A is for 0.05 mol, C for 0.15 mol, D for 0.2 mol.

calculation_steps: Mole ratio Zn:H\u2082 = 1:1, so moles H\u2082 = 0.1 mol
At STP, volume H\u2082 = 0.1 mol \u00d7 22.4 L/mol = 2.24 L

source_trace.content_anchor: Mass-volume problems: convert mass to moles, use mole ratio, convert to volume at STP

explanations_tiered.definition: Mass-volume stoichiometry combines mole ratios from balanced equations with molar volume (22.4 L/mol at STP).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Convert given mass to moles, use mole ratio to find moles of gas, then convert to volume at STP using 22.4 L/mol.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Zinc reacts with acid to make hydrogen gas. 65 g of zinc (1 mole) makes 22.4 L of hydrogen at STP. 6.5 g is 0.1 mole, so it makes 2.24 L of hydrogen.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Moles Zn = \(\frac{6.5}{65} = 0.1\) mol. From \(\mathrm{Zn + 2HCl \rightarrow ZnCl_2 + H_2}\), mole ratio Zn:H\u2082 = 1:1, so moles H\u2082 = 0.1 mol. At STP, volume = 0.1 mol \u00d7 22.4 L/mol = 2.24 L.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Dimensional analysis: \(6.5\text{ g Zn} \times \frac{1\text{ mol Zn}}{65\text{ g Zn}} \times \frac{1\text{ mol H}_2}{1\text{ mol Zn}} \times \frac{22.4\text{ L}}{1\text{ mol H}_2} = 2.24\text{ L H}_2\).
worked_solution: Step 1: Balanced: \(\mathrm{Zn + 2HCl \rightarrow ZnCl_2 + H_2}\)
Step 2: Moles Zn = 6.5 g / 65 g/mol = 0.1 mol
Step 3: From equation, 1 mol Zn produces 1 mol H\u2082
Step 4: Moles H\u2082 = 0.1 mol
Step 5: Volume at STP = 0.1 mol \u00d7 22.4 L/mol = 2.24 L
reasoning_steps: Use mole ratio to find moles of H\u2082
Convert moles H\u2082 to volume at STP (22.4 L/mol)

formula_analysis.name: Single displacement of hydrogen by zinc

misconceptions: Using the volume of Zn instead of H\u2082 (Zn is solid, not gas, so no molar volume).
Forgetting that 1 mol gas = 22.4 L only at STP (standard temperature and pressure).

glossary_entry.definition: 6.5 g Zn produces 2.24 L H\u2082 at STP based on 1:1 mole ratio and molar volume.

flashcard.front: What volume of H\u2082 at STP from 6.5 g Zn in \(\mathrm{Zn + 2HCl \rightarrow ZnCl_2 + H_2}\)?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What volume of H\u2082 at STP from 6.5 g Zn in \(\mathrm{Zn + 2HCl \rightarrow ZnCl_2 + H_2}\)?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Calculating volume of gas produced from mass of reactant using mole ratios and molar volume.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Lab demonstration: Zn + HCl produces H\u2082 gas; collect over water and measure volume.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. 6.5 g Zn = 0.1 mol, produces 0.1 mol H\u2082 = 2.24 L at STP.
